concerns.text: The plan validates model performance using 5-fold cross-validation and bootstrapping on the SAME dataset used to engineer features and train the model. In materials science with small datasets (<50 points), this creates a circular validation where the 'validation' target is not independent of the training process. The F-test compares the model to a null mean, but if the features are derived from the same composition data that defines the target's physical context, the statistical significance may reflect overfitting to noise rather than a genuine physical relationship. A true validation requires a held-out test set (external to the training CV) or, preferably, external experimental data not used in feature engineering.

The descriptors (VEC, electronegativity, atomic radii) are deterministic functions of the chemical composition. The target variables (coercivity, saturation magnetization) are physical properties of the *same* composition. While not tautological, the relationship is heavily confounded by microstructure (grain size, defects, phase purity) which is NOT included as a predictor. The plan acknowledges this in FR-010 but proceeds to model the composition-property link as if it were the primary driver. Without controlling for microstructure (e.g., by grouping data by synthesis method or excluding heterogeneous datasets), the model may learn spurious correlations between composition and hysteresis that are actually driven by unmeasured processing variables. This risks a 'result that is true by definition' of the dataset's bias rather than a physical law.

The plan proposes an F-test to compare the model against a null model (mean prediction). With a small sample size (<50) and high-dimensional feature space (even with 5 descriptors, the ratio is poor), the F-test has low power and is highly sensitive to violations of normality and homoscedasticity, which are common in materials data. Bootstrapping 1000 resamples on <50 points may not provide stable confidence intervals for R² if the underlying distribution is skewed. The methodology is statistically fragile for the stated data regime.
